speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two, January 3, 1996, interview with Mr. Moses Goldberg. You were telling me about the rule-- some of the rules that the Germans set for the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. As I mentioned, we were not able to pray in the synagogue. The synagogue was practically closed. But we prayed in the neighborhood homes of certain people, which was illegal, too. There was somebody standing outside and watching if nobody is coming. It was also like we have a curfew, curfew, right? At, like, 6:00, a Jew was not able to go-- walk on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where we lived, a few families-- you know, we were youngsters. So we got together, and we did all kind of-- to occupy ourselves, to be busy with something. So we did some-- played some kind of games, played cards. But if they caught somebody in the streets, they usually beat him up very badly. And everybody was afraid to go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: We also had to wear the armbands with these-- with the Jewish Magen David. And a Jew could not walk around in the street during the day without it. And also, we were not allowed to walk in the main streets. When we wanted to go from one place or the other, we had to go through the side streets. We were not allowed to go. And if you walked on the sidewalk and a German came, we had to go out of the sidewalk and walk on the gutter. So we had to make room for them. They, they made us feel like, like dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the Polish people from your town, how were they acting to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were happy about it. They, they, they, they were so happy about what happens to the Jews. They were laughing from us. They were seeing, seeing us working all kind of dirty work. And they said, it's good for you. You had it so good all those years. Now you have to see what-- how you have to do it. And they were happy the Germans came in. Not once-- they spit in our faces. We couldn't do noth-- anything about it. This is the Polish way. As I mentioned before, they were very big antisemites. And they were waiting for the thing too, what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Christian neighbors that showed any acts of kindness?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were, but not too many. Not-- my best friend-- and I had a very good friend who, he was Christian. But the parents, they, they became like, like volksdeutsche. Volksdeutsche means they were like descendant of German, Germans. And he didn't want to play with me anymore, didn't want to go out with me anymore, because I was a Jew. And as this-- as a little boy, we used to play in their garden. And we used to-- I mean, I couldn't eat there, but he knew all the Jewish songs and all the everything in Jewish. But later, he became so estranged, that-- like never, never knew me before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the mood of the Jewish men and women? When you used to gather in somebody's house, what did they used to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to talk about the war was going to be. We hear things. We heard the people were, were killed there and there. But we didn't know that time yet about the, the [NON-ENGLISH], which is people-- they send-- they said they're sending away for work. But actually we didn't know what's happening yet. Only we knew what happens in town. There was some beating and shooting, too. But actually, this Final Solution, what's going to happen, we didn't know. We were just praying and hoping that the war should end. We should be able to survive the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have such a big bar mitzvah, because I was bar mitzvahed right in 1939, when the war broke out. So the Germans came in September. And my birthday was Hanukkah, which was December. It was-- it was-- I went to shul in the morning with my father. And we, we brought some cake and whiskey. And everybody was singing 'Le Chaim.' And on the Shabbat, I had-- I was called to the Torah. And I was saying the haftorah from Hanukkah. I still remember it was beginning with [NON-ENGLISH]. But this was the whole thing. This was the whole of my bar mitzvah. Actually, at home, we didn't make those bar mitzvahs like they make here. They just made small for the family, some gathering or thing. But, but in the war, there wasn't-- it was-- I was happy that I was able to be with my father together and, and to pray together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me when a ghetto was formed in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, they starting to clean up the big cities, the big streets where Jews lived, where they had their stores. And they put them in, in, in smaller streets in the outskirts of the town. The stores that people have, they used to put German [NON-ENGLISH], which they used to run the store for you. Actually, they were the owners of the store. You had nothing more to say. They had the keys from the store and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whoever had some money would still be able to buy some bread on the black market, because we had rations at that time. And a lot of people had nothing more to eat. Because it was very hard to survive on the rations. And not everybody had money to, to buy. People used to sell their clothing. They used to sell their possessions to buy food. And it was very hard, hard times that time. So they began putting the Jews in certain designated areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They put them in certain streets. And also, they put prohibition of, of going out in the evening again. They made it a little bit earlier not to leave. But we were not walled in. It was an open-- like an open ghetto. But like in [INAUDIBLE], a bigger town, they had a ghetto there with walls on. So you had to go-- when you had to go out, you had to have a permission or something. But we still lived there, but it was still oppressed by the Germans. They used to do all kind of tricks with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that point, they moved in some other Jewish families, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought them from all over the-- from the outskirts of our town. They come from [? Stadla. ?] I have a very good friend here who lives in New Jersey. He lived-- his name is Henry Sperling. He lived in [? Stadla ?], which is about-- to walk-- about an hour or 45 minutes. They brought all the Jews to the town. They brought even from my wife's hometown. She's from [INAUDIBLE]. I had a few people who lived-- [INAUDIBLE] was a resort town. And they cleaned it up earlier because they put the Germans-- all the German officers used to come for-- you know, to have a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they scattered the people over. Then the people come from Piwniczna, which is also-- from [INAUDIBLE], from all over. And now-- and if you had two, three rooms, you had to take in the other family. So you were very, very squashed in, in, in an apartment. You sometimes could live two, three families together. And about how many families in all lived in this ghetto at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: The families, I would say about 200 families. And there was-- there was a big, big amount of people there. Because they had a lot of children. The younger people had children and everything. So it was a hard life, very hard life. But we still survive, because we had the families together. So everybody was doing the best he can.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like, when I was-- in 1941, I was already-- they were already taking me to work. I was working in, in a-- in a quarry, in a [GERMAN] they called in German, where they'd take out stones from, from a wall-- from a mountain. I used to go there in the morning and come back at night. And they used to-- already starting to send away people to all kind of, of jobs. One day, it was in May 1942, the Germans came from Nowy [? Sacz ?] and they came to the Judenrat. We had the Judenrat. They were providing work for the Germans and everything. And they said they need 100 people to be sent to a town what they called Rabka. I was working for, for a Christian guy who was making certain kind of shoes. And they-- till that time, they couldn't touch me to go to work, because it was essential to work for the Germans. So I had, like, a-- I had to work for him. I didn't get paid. But I was happy, because I was home. I was going home every evening. But when they came in for the 100 people, there was very few people left. So they took already the people who had all kinds of jobs. So I was on their list to go to Rabka, too. So we went. I remember, they took 100 people from my hometown, married men, with-- older people, younger people my age. They needed 100 people. They had to get-- the Judenrat had-- they were merciless. They took anybody who had five children, six children, they took the men away. I remember we-- it was in the evening when we were going to the railroad station, were standing, and all the families came to say goodbye. My father, he came, too. [SOBBING] And he told me, don't volunteer for any work. Whatever happens, don't volunteer. So I was very young at that time. I was about 15 years old. So we, we said goodbye. And I didn't know I were to see him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took us with a train to Rabka. What was the work there? There was, a-- they called it the [NON-ENGLISH]. What do they do? They took the Ukrainians. You know, the Ukraine was a part of Russia. And when the Germans came in, they, they promised the Ukraine that when the war will be ended, they'll give them their country for their own. They won't belong to Russia. So they cooperated with the Germans. So they took the young boys, the 19, 20 years, and they taught them how to be SS men. That's what the school was. There were about 200 Ukrainian. And there were about 50 SS men. And they taught them how to shoot and how to become SS men. They were wearing the SS uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were living in the-- see, Rabka was a resort town before the war. People used to go there for all kind of sickness. So we lived in-- like, in three villas. A villa could have, like, 30, 30 rooms. So we were 100 people-- were, were living in three villas in the outside or outskirts of the town. And they make us come every morning to work on that-- in that school. The first day we came, they shaved our heads completely. We-- and, and they told us whoever cannot work, who was sick, who was old, who was too young to work, they should come out of the-- of the line and report. Of course, nobody wanted to go, because they knew what's going to be-- it was a very bad camp. I remember the first day, we were working in the-- what, what our job was to do is to make a [NON-ENGLISH]. This is how they should teach them how to shoot. It was a big walk-- it was a big mountain, and we had to straighten out the mountain like this. And they should-- they make all kinds of preparations to make-- they should be able to teach them how to shoot. But the conditions in Rabka was very bad. There was one man, the, the commandant from the camp. His name was Rosen-- Wilhelm Rosenbaum. He was a sadist. I have never seen anybody like this. He, he was walking around with a-- with a [INAUDIBLE], with the-- you know, from leather what you--

speaker: interviewer
text: The whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: A whip, right. On the end of the whip, he had a metal thing. And he was hitting from the back, it came under your eye. So most of the people had black eyes from [INAUDIBLE]. And women, he was merciless. And he found anybody, a Jew with the name Rosenbaum, he, he shot the whole family. He was going around with a gun. And he was going around-- if somebody did something wrong, he put his name down. And when there was an [NON-ENGLISH, you know, he was one of the-- I have a friend whose name is Stanley Appell. He's from my hometown. He's a very good friend. And he, he was going with-- he did some kind of work that the guy didn't like. So he put his name, name on the list. And he, he was scared. He says, I'm finished. They'll call me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they, they, they made [NON-ENGLISH], when they shoot people, he said I'm healthy. I could work. He took a big thing. And he so he let him go. He-- he's alive. He lives in New Jersey. We are very good friends. So what happened in Rabka, we, we did all kind of work, all kind of manual, heavy work from the morning till the night, rain or shine. We came there, in, in-- it was already in fall. So it was very bitter cold, and we had to stay on, on-- and do all kind of works. They used to bring monuments from the Jewish cemeteries. And we used to make an appellplatz for the-- for the Germans to stand there. So you can see all the matzevahs with the names. There's some-- there were some black granite. And there were all kind. We had to put them down like this. We had no other choice.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did all kind of work there. We, we made roads. Usually here, you see, when they make roads, they have a big such a thing where they split the road. It was everything by, like, a car with a-- with a motor. We had to pull it ourselves. We were 20 people to pull one thing. It was so hard. And I remember, there was one, one man-- we made a song. You had to-- you had to make something to entertain yourself. So there was a black, black road. And we were pulling the thing, and he was singing, [NON- ENGLISH]. And as we're pulling the, the heavy thing-- I don't know if you know what I mean. This was a straight thing to straighten out the roads. And we worked-- were very hard at it. And there from time to time, they made [INAUDIBLE]. They killed people. They brought from Nowy [? Sacz, ?] a group maybe of 80 Jews, [INAUDIBLE] Jews. And, and our people had to dig the graves in the forest, which was right after there. And in the evening, they took the people there. And I was passing by, and I saw one of the groups was my rabbi from my-- from my other town. And he looked at me. I never forget the look in his eyes, like, help me. I was helpless. I couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was-- I just forgot the name. It will come back to me. What they did in the evening, they took the whole group. We finished the, the graves. I didn't go to the grave, because I was the youngest. They, they, they were taking care of me, so I shouldn't see it. But the friend of mine-- there were about 10 boys who went to the grave. And one of them was digging the grave. And the grave was finished. They said, hop up. Come out of the grave. One boy, a friend of mine, couldn't, couldn't-- he was not built like this to jump up. So the guard that's the SS man say, stay there. And he took a gun and shot him there. And they brought the people there to the forest. And they had loudspeakers. You know what a loudspeaker is. And they, they was playing songs. They played German songs, [GERMAN] It's all over. Everything is finished. That's how the Jews had to go to their graves. They were standing in front of the graves. And each one was shot. And they fall, one after the other. And then we had to come back, and we put-- to cover them up. And sometimes the ground was still moving. Because the people were not completely dead. This, this was the first camp that we were. It was the most horrible camp that I remember. They took from our people, they took about-- from the 100 people, about 30 people. They shot them. And at home, we did-- they didn't know who was alive, who was not alive. So We had to make a list of the people who are alive. And then the family, they saw who was-- and there were women with five, six children when their husbands died. And then they shot them for-- just because they didn't like their faces or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about these [INAUDIBLE] while you lived in this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- almost every week, there were [INAUDIBLE]. They were shooting people. They brought people from other towns. And they had fun. They told them to get undressed, the women. When they found somebody on, on-- hidden, Jews were hidden, they put them in, in the basement in the-- in the-- it was like a jail. And they kept them there for a day or two. And then they, they shoot them all. They put them all in the, in the, in the graves. There are-- there are graves in Rabka. A friend of mine was just there two years ago. He brought me pictures. They have some kind of monuments. And it-- and my wish is to go there once. My home friends that I went to school with [INAUDIBLE]. And nobody knows about it, because it's not the famous camp. Nobody heard about Rabka. But people who know, who were there, know what kind of a camp it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the names of your friends and townspeople who died at Rabka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was-- first, there was named Weiss. And then was Itzchy Shermer. And there was-- one-- there were older people than me. His name was-- I just can't remember all the names right now. But they were very good friends of mine. They, they were-- they're laying dead in the grave. And unfortunately, nobody goes there. Now, you go to-- got to Belzec, you go there. But it's a small town. And I, I saw the movie, my friend was there. And he took the home movie of the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, I was-- you see, what happened at camp-- that-- every, every-- once a week, they allowed seven people to go home to their families just for the night and come back the next day. Who did they choose first? They choose the, the husbands who have wives and children, that-- they want they should go and see their children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those seven people came back-- they used to go, like, Saturday afternoon and came back Sunday night. And they used to bring food. Every family sent food for the other people. So they brought a lot of food for everybody, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't go home so fast, because I was single. So first, they took the family people. When I went-- when my turn came, I went home. And it was like, you know, coming back to reality. And my father was home. My sister was home. And they gave me to eat and everything. And I went to see my-- parents of my other friends who were not with us anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like coming home. But it was so short. It was just one, one, one night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next time my turn was, my-- I got a letter from my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my sister writes me the letter in Polish. I still remember. She writes, I have bad news to tell you. [SOBBING] We lost our father. But he didn't suffer too much. What was the story? Like I told you-- like I told this story before, every Tuesday, the Jews in Stary Sacz had to go and, and stay in front of them to take them to all kind of work. So one man, he was lame on one leg. He had a shorter leg. He was going with his wife to the place. He was passing our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German-- a German man saw him from far away. They were coming. He says, SS man. He says, stop. Stop. And he didn't want to stop. He was afraid. He ran into our house. He came to our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father asked him, what happened? Why you running like this? He says, nothing, nothing. He went in and hid in the closet. A minute later, the Germans came to the hall and asked, where does the Jews live here? Where do they live? There was a small house. So the Christians said, here. This is the door where they live. He came in. He asked, [GERMAN], where's the Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father wouldn't tell where, where he's hidden. And he says, I don't know. So he looked all over. He opened the closet. And he took him out of the closet. First, he beat him up. And then he took him and my father in the back yard and told them to lay down and shot them. [SOBBING] My sister told me later my father's last words were [NON-ENGLISH] And my sister and his wife had to stay by the window and see how they're being shot. Of course, we made the minyan in, in Rabka. We had another few people who said Kaddish. We were able to say-- I wasn't able to sit shiva because I had to go to work the next day. But I was able-- I was able to say Kaddish for about six months, as long as we were in, in, in Rabka. Then, then I was-- I was left there already, just with my sister. My sister was home by herself. And she got sick. And they took her to the hospital. And a day before-- a day before they, they took the people from Stary Sacz to, to the bigger town-- there was the [NON-ENGLISH] They let the whole camp to go home from Rabka to Stary Sacz. I came to four empty walls. [SOBBING] There was nobody home anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I slept by myself, and I took with me whatever I could, you know, from the stuff in the house, lady stuff, and I was able later to sell it to the Christians and, and help myself with food. But this was the last time the people saw their families. They took us. They, they, they went with us to the train. Can you imagine a wife and children saying goodbye to their husbands and they, they knew they wouldn't see them anymore? Because the next day was the-- they took them all out to Nowy Sacz. From there, they sent them to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was such crying there, such a thing that-- unbelievable. Children and, and wives and parents was, was-- and we were already the lucky one, because we were in camp. At that time was-- the worst thing, they took us to camp. But we were lucky ones, we were in camp, that we still have-- because we had to work, and we would be alive. But then we found out that they took them to Nowy Sacz. And then from there, they took them to Treblinka or to Belzec, all kind of thing. And I never heard from my sister anymore. And this was the end of, of my family. Also, my other sisters were scattered somewhere else. And nobody knew where they are. My brothers were also in, in the part of Russia there. And then the Germans came. They took him. But we work, still, in Rabka. I remember the, the people-- the people-- we were all shaved. The heads were all shaved. And we had to work in the heat. Some people, their heads swell up, not only the heads, but the eyes. They couldn't see how they work. But they had to go every day to work. And they could not wear anything on their heads, because we had to work just like that. And we worked till, till 1943. We were in Rabka. We finished that [NON-ENGLISH]. It's still standing now. People see it, what work we did there. We, we-- from a-- day and night, we worked on it under all kind of conditions, without clothes, without anything. But I remember still on Yom Kippur, the first Yom Kippur we were in Rabka, we still-- the first-- they let us have a minyan on, on Yom Kippur night. But Yom Kippur morning, when we came back for lunch, we went to the kitchen with our-- with our-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH]. We had little pots. Everybody had a pot. And we made believe that we get the lunch. But nobody took lunch. The cook-- the one who was the cook was spooning it with, with a big thing, but there was nothing there. We didn't eat. We still, still fasted the first Yom Kippur. And then in the evening, we were able to eat something. But this was only the first Yom Kippur we fasted. The other one-- we fasted all the time. But you had to have something, black coffee or something. But this, I remember that Yom Kippur. I never forget it. People still, still believed. And the people who even weren't religious believed that there's a God and he's going to help us one, one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were working in Rabka. One day, they, they-- in the middle of the work, they, they took us. They told us we're going-- they taking away from Rabka. Now you can't go back to your room. Whatever you have there, it stays there. And this-- you're going just like this. I still had my tefillin with me, which I was putting on every day. But this was already gone, too. They took us on, on, on their wagons, on the truck loads. And they took us-- they took us to, to Plaszow.
